title: Stress and Strain
subtitle: Stress and strain for Class 11 Physics (NCERT) — definitions, types and units

blocks.heading: The Idea
blocks.body:
When a force acts on a solid, the solid deforms. Two quantities describe this:
Stress is the restoring force per unit area set up inside the material to oppose the deformation. It has the same units as pressure (Pa = N/m²).
Strain is the fractional change in dimension — it is dimensionless.
There are three types, depending on how the force is applied:
Tensile / compressive — force normal to surface → elongation or compression
Shear — force parallel to surface → angular deformation
Bulk (volumetric) — pressure applied on all sides → volume change

blocks.heading: Stress:
blocks.body:
Longitudinal strain:
Shear strain:
Volumetric strain:
All strains are dimensionless ratios.

blocks.heading: Real-World Example
blocks.body: Steel cable in a lift: A lift cable carries a load of 2000 kg. If the cable has cross-section 2 cm², the stress is \sigma = mg/A = 2000 \times 10 / (2 \times 10^{-4}) = 10^8 Pa = 100 MPa. Engineers design cables to stay well below the yield stress (~250 MPa for mild steel) with a safety factor of 2–4.

blocks.heading: Try It At Home
blocks.body: Hang a 500 g mass from a rubber band and measure how much it stretches. Measure the band's original length and cross-sectional area. Calculate the tensile stress and longitudinal strain. How do they change if you double the load?

blocks.heading: What If…
blocks.body: What if you applied equal forces inward on all six faces of a steel cube simultaneously? The stress is bulk (volumetric) — every solid has a bulk modulus that resists this. Try to find the bulk modulus of steel and calculate how much a 1 cm³ cube compresses under deep ocean pressure (~600 atm).

quiz.question: A wire of length 2 m and cross-section 1 mm² is stretched by 2 mm under a force of 200 N. What is the tensile stress?
quiz.explanation: Stress = F/A = 200 / (1×10⁻⁶) = 2×10⁸ Pa. Note: 1 mm² = 10⁻⁶ m².
